figures.caption: TABLE II: Quantitative comparison on the Nymeria test dataset and Self-Collected test dataset. The “Data Composition” column indicates the datasets used for training. ↓ indicates lower is better, ↑ indicates higher is better. The best results are highlighted in bold.
Fig. 6: Failure Case Analysis for Box Moving, Sofa Sitting, and Ball Kicking.
TABLE IX: Motion Difficulty Grading System.

Fig. 4: Real-world interaction on Unitree G1. (a) Few-shot Generalization: The robot performs instructions, successfully adapting to varied obstacle layouts and shifted sofa positions. (b) Zero-shot Capabilities: The robot executes ”Sit on the chair” and ”walk towards the sofa”, interacting with the object despite the complete absence of the chair data during training.

Fig. 3: Detailed architecture of ZeroWBC. The framework operates in two stages: (a) Multimodal Motion Generation: We use a VQ-VAE and fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL to predict motion tokens from initial images and text instructions, decoding them into continuous human motions. (b) General Motion Tracking: Generated motions are retargeted and tracked by an RL-based policy. A curriculum learning strategy with increasing difficulty ensures robust tracking performance.
Fig. 5: An example of Fine-tuning Samples
